metadata.title: Pre-ICO Frontend Security Audit
metadata.auditor: Senior Blockchain Security Auditor (Claude Code, fresh pass)

findings.title: User profile cache TTL (15 min) delays role-revocation propagation
findings.risk: If the backend revokes an admin role, the client continues to honor the cached profile for up to 15 minutes. During that window a demoted user can still render admin-only UI affordances. Middleware re-checks on navigation, but the UX-level privilege gap is a phishing/social-engineering vector.
findings.fix: Reduce TTL to <=5 min, invalidate the cache on every successful /api/auth/me poll, and listen for a server-sent 'role-changed' event or BroadcastChannel message to hard-clear sessionStorage immediately.

findings.title: Unvalidated amountEth input passed to parseEther
findings.excerpt: value = parseEther(amountEth); // amountEth flows in from user input
findings.risk: parseEther throws on malformed strings and silently truncates beyond 18 decimals. No min/max bounds are enforced client-side, so UX is poor and fat-finger contributions (e.g. '1000' instead of '1') are not warned about before the wallet prompt. Contract min/max only revert after the user has paid gas.
findings.fix: Add explicit validation: reject non-numeric, negative, zero, >18 decimals, or amounts outside [MIN_CONTRIBUTION, MAX_CONTRIBUTION] before calling parseEther. Surface a human-readable error and require explicit confirmation above a soft cap.

findings.title: Axios client has no timeout, no retry, no abort, no 401 deduplication
findings.excerpt: const api = axios.create({ baseURL: API_URL, withCredentials: true });
findings.risk: Requests can hang indefinitely on a slow/unreachable backend, freezing the UI. Concurrent 401s from expired sessions all trigger logout redirects and can loop. No AbortSignal means React unmounts leak in-flight requests that may later resolve and write to stale state.
findings.fix: Set timeout: 30000. Add a response interceptor that funnels 401s through a single in-flight Promise (deduplicate). Expose an AbortController per hook call. Exponential-backoff retry for idempotent GETs only.

findings.title: Admin role fetched from backend inside middleware with no timeout
findings.risk: If the backend is slow or partitioned, middleware blocks every protected request. A malformed/empty JSON response falls through to null, which is treated as non-admin (fail-closed for privilege but denies legitimate admins). No caching means every navigation hits the backend.
findings.fix: Use AbortSignal.timeout(3000) on the fetch; on timeout/malformed body, return 503 for admin routes and fall back to a cached role (signed, short-TTL cookie) for non-admin routes. Memoize per session id for 60 s.

findings.title: Contract addresses silently fall back to the zero address in production
findings.risk: A missing NEXT_PUBLIC_TOKEN_SALE_ADDRESS / NEXT_PUBLIC_VESTING_ADDRESS in production only logs a warning. The UI keeps rendering the contribution form; transactions fail at submit and can be misinterpreted as a deliberate zero-address send.
findings.fix: In next.config.mjs throw at build time when NODE_ENV==='production' and either address is missing or not a valid 0x-prefixed 20-byte hex. At runtime, assertContractConfigured() should be called before rendering write-capable UI, not only before writeContract.

findings.title: WalletConnect project ID is shipped in the client bundle
findings.risk: All NEXT_PUBLIC_* values are public by design, but WalletConnect IDs are rate-limited per project. A scraped ID can be abused to exhaust the project's quota or to observe relay metadata. Not a credential, but should be rotatable.
findings.fix: Document rotation procedure in .env.local.example, add a gitleaks pre-commit hook, monitor WalletConnect analytics for abuse. Consider proxying relay traffic through a backend.

findings.title: Client does not rate-limit SIWE nonce fetches
findings.risk: Backend must be the real line of defense, but a client-side guard prevents accidental self-DoS (e.g., React StrictMode double-invoke + retry loops) and surfaces backoff UI consistently.
findings.fix: Throttle fetchSiweNonce to <=1/5 s per address; cache the active nonce for 60 s; surface a backoff UI if the backend responds 429.

findings.title: sanitizeText is a no-op on the server
findings.risk: Any future SSR/RSC path that feeds user-controlled strings into a non-JSX sink (CSV export, Content-Disposition, dangerouslySetInnerHTML in a server component) bypasses sanitization entirely. React escaping currently saves us but the contract is fragile.
findings.fix: Replace the server branch with isomorphic-dompurify or a minimal strip-tags implementation so sanitization is always enforced. Add a unit test for the SSR path.

findings.title: Data-export action is not audit-logged
findings.excerpt: const blob = new Blob([content], { type: mimeType }); /* download */
findings.risk: GDPR Art. 15/30 require the data controller to keep a log of access and export events. The frontend triggers the export but does not notify the backend of a successful download, leaving the audit trail incomplete.
findings.fix: POST an audit/data-export event to the backend (user id, timestamp, row count, format, success flag) before triggering the blob download. Store immutably for at least the statutory retention window.

findings.title: CSRF header attached only if cookie exists; no pre-flight nonce fetch
findings.risk: If the XSRF-TOKEN cookie is missing (first visit, third-party cookie stripping, Safari ITP), the state-changing request goes out without an X-XSRF-TOKEN header. The backend must reject such requests, but the frontend should never rely on backend behavior alone.
findings.fix: On app bootstrap, call GET /api/csrf to seed the token. Fail state-changing requests client-side if the token is absent. Add a Vitest test asserting this.

findings.title: CSV export does not neutralize formula-injection prefixes
findings.excerpt: const escape = (val) => { if (str.includes(',') || str.includes('"') || str.includes('\n')) { return `"${str.replace(/"/g, '""')}`; } return str; };
findings.risk: Fields sourced from user-controlled data (bio, notes, handle, full_name) can begin with =, +, -, @, tab (\t) or carriage-return (\r). When the exported CSV is opened in Excel / Google Sheets / LibreOffice those prefixes are interpreted as spreadsheet formulas, enabling attacks such as =HYPERLINK / =WEBSERVICE / DDE command execution that exfiltrate the victim's row data to an attacker-controlled URL. This is classic CWE-1236 formula injection and is particularly serious for GDPR-driven data-export endpoints.
findings.fix: Before quoting, if the first character of a field is one of `= + - @ \t \r`, prefix it with a single apostrophe (') so spreadsheets treat the cell as text. Quote EVERY field unconditionally, not only when it contains a delimiter, so leading whitespace cannot hide a formula prefix. Add a unit test in lib/__tests__/export.test.ts that asserts =1+1 round-trips to '=1+1 and that a bio of '@SUM(A1)' is neutralized.

findings.title: Path-traversal segments (..) are not explicitly rejected
findings.risk: Router normalization already neutralises /.., but explicit rejection is clearer intent and defends against a future router change.
findings.fix: Add: if (path.split('/').some(seg => seg === '..')) return false; with a dedicated unit test.

findings.title: Block-explorer URL is hardcoded to Etherscan mainnet
findings.risk: If EXPECTED_CHAIN_ID is switched to a testnet or L2, the user is linked to the wrong explorer. Not a security vuln, but erodes trust.
findings.fix: Map chain id -> explorer base URL in lib/web3/config.ts and build the link from EXPECTED_CHAIN_ID.

findings.title: No AbortController on the bootstrap /api/auth/me fetch
findings.risk: React StrictMode double-invokes effects; the first fetch races with the second and can set stale state. The boolean flag mitigates the state write but not the network waste.
findings.fix: Use AbortController and pass its signal to fetch; abort in the cleanup function.

findings.title: useUserContribution collapses loading + zero into a single state
findings.excerpt: return { contribution: data ?? BigInt(0), isLoading, refetch };
findings.risk: If the RPC is degraded and returns undefined, UI displays '0 contributed' when the correct message is 'unknown; please retry'.
findings.fix: Return { contribution, isLoading, error } and let the component distinguish the three states.

findings.title: No explicit Cache-Control for SRI-protected bundles
findings.risk: A stale CDN edge could continue serving an older hash after rotation, producing SRI-mismatch errors for users.
findings.fix: Add a headers() rule for /_next/static/* enforcing Cache-Control: public, max-age=31536000, immutable, and document the CDN purge step in the deploy runbook.

findings.title: Cookie-consent state lives in localStorage (not server-auditable)
findings.risk: Non-sensitive, but GDPR Art. 7(1) asks controllers to demonstrate consent. A user who clears localStorage leaves no server-side trace that consent was ever given.
findings.fix: Mirror the consent payload to the backend on acceptance (POST /api/consent) and issue a signed cookie so the server can prove consent on audit.

findings.title: Admin whitelist input not validated through parseContributionAmount
findings.excerpt: const amount = Number(amountStr); if (Number.isNaN(amount) || amount <= 0) {...} entries.push({ address, maxContribution: parseEther(amountStr) });
findings.risk: The audit-fixed contribution path (H-02) routes user-supplied ETH amounts through `parseContributionAmount`, which rejects malformed strings, scientific notation, leading +/-, hex, and any value with more than 18 fractional digits before they reach `parseEther`. The admin whitelist path bypasses that helper entirely: it pre-validates with `Number(amountStr) > 0` (which silently coerces large or precision-losing values) and then hands the original string to `parseEther`, which silently truncates beyond 18 decimals and throws on inputs `Number()` accepts (e.g. `1e18`). An admin who pastes a fat-finger CSV (`0x…, 100000000000000000000.5`) loses precision; an unsupported notation reverts the on-chain transaction and burns gas. Same bug class as H-02, scoped to admin role.
findings.fix: Replace the inline `Number(amountStr) > 0` + `parseEther(amountStr)` pair with the existing `parseContributionAmount` validator (or a new `parseWhitelistAmount` helper that delegates to the same regex / decimal-bound rules) so all wei-bound user input on the frontend shares one validation contract. Add a Vitest spec that asserts `1e18`, `100.0000000000000000001`, and ` 1` are all rejected for the whitelist path.

findings.title: 11 moderate npm advisories pending wagmi/MetaMask major upgrade
findings.excerpt: 11 moderate severity vulnerabilities — postcss CWE-79 (XSS via unescaped </style>), uuid weakness via @metamask/utils, transitive in @rainbow-me/rainbowkit
findings.risk: Two distinct chains: (1) postcss <8.5.10 ships an XSS sink (GHSA-qx2v-qp2m-jg93) reachable via Next.js' bundled postcss; impact is build-time only on this codebase but the advisory still flags as production because postcss is not in `devDependencies`. (2) The MetaMask SDK transitive chain (`@metamask/utils` → `uuid`, `@metamask/rpc-errors` → `@metamask/utils`) propagates a uuid weakness up to `@wagmi/connectors` and `@rainbow-me/rainbowkit`. None are directly exploitable in the deployed bundle today (CSP `script-src 'self'` + Trusted Types blocks DOM-XSS sinks, and the wallet SDK chain runs server-isolated until the user opts to connect), but they remain unpatched moderate findings against the production dependency tree and an investor or independent auditor will flag them.
findings.fix: Track the upstream releases (`@metamask/sdk` ≥ next minor, `wagmi` ≥ next minor, `@rainbow-me/rainbowkit` ≥ next compatible release) and bump as soon as they ship. Until then: (a) tighten the CI npm-audit gate to `--audit-level=moderate --omit=dev` once upstream fixes are available, (b) add a weekly Dependabot grouping rule for the MetaMask + wagmi family, (c) document the residual risk and the compensating CSP/Trusted-Types posture in `SECURITY.md`.

findings.title: 401 interceptor redirects to /login without preserving the requested path
findings.risk: Middleware forwards the originally-requested path through `?redirect=` when an unauthenticated user hits a protected route. The axios 401 handler fires when a session expires *during* an authenticated session and discards that context — the user is dropped on /login with no way back to the page they were on, which is a noticeable UX regression and erodes trust in long-running sessions.
findings.fix: Compute `const next = encodeURIComponent(window.location.pathname + window.location.search)` and navigate to `/login?redirect=${next}` only when the current path passes `isSafeRedirectTarget`. Add a Vitest test that asserts an off-origin pathname collapses to `/login` without the parameter.

findings.title: CSP-report rate limiter trusts unverified X-Forwarded-For
findings.risk: The endpoint is rate-limited at 10 reports/min keyed on the value of `X-Forwarded-For`. On Vercel / Railway the platform overwrites these headers, but a misconfigured deployment behind a different proxy (or a direct-to-origin probe) could let an attacker spoof a unique IP per request and exhaust the in-memory limiter, or worse — fill it to evict legitimate IPs. CSP reports do leak path / line-number information, so abuse is not free of impact.
findings.fix: Honour `x-forwarded-for` only when the request arrived via a known reverse-proxy hop (e.g. validate against `process.env.TRUSTED_PROXY_CIDRS`), and fall back to the immediate connection IP otherwise. Document the deployment requirement in `docs/SECURITY_CONTACTS.md`. Optionally promote the rate limiter store to Redis (the hook already exists) so a per-instance memory map is not the enforcement boundary.

findings.title: Influencer name and handle bypass sanitizeText defense-in-depth
findings.risk:
React JSX escaping prevents direct DOM-XSS via these fields, and the strict CSP (`script-src 'self'`, Trusted Types) is the real backstop. However the codebase enforces a `sanitizeText` defense-in-depth wrapper on `bio`, `notes`, `message_sent`, and `response` — the same trust assumption (these strings flow from a third-party social-media scraper) applies to `full_name` and `handle`. The asymmetric coverage is a future-proofing gap: if a downstream component ever swaps a `

{full_name}

` for `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` (or a future Server Component path emits the value into a non-JSX sink), the missing sanitizer becomes a real XSS sink.
findings.fix: Wrap `full_name` and `handle` (and `niche`) in `sanitizeText(...)` everywhere they are rendered, mirroring the treatment of `bio`. Add a tiny `` component that does this once so future authors cannot forget. Add a Vitest spec that asserts a payload like `` round-trips through `sanitizeText` to plain text.

verdict.summary: Fresh-pass audit (2026-04-25): all 22 findings (5H/8M/9L) are now closed in code. The previous remediation pass closed 17 (5H/6M/6L); this pass closes the remaining 5 (M-07: admin whitelist now routes through `parseWhitelistAmount`, sharing the H-02 regex/decimal-bound validator; M-08: Dependabot grouping for the wagmi/MetaMask family + documented residual posture in SECURITY.md, awaiting upstream fixes before tightening the moderate npm-audit gate; L-07: 401 axios interceptor preserves the requested path via `?redirect=` validated through `isSafeRedirectTarget`; L-08: csp-report rate limiter only honours `x-forwarded-for` when behind a trusted reverse proxy and otherwise collapses to a single global bucket; L-09: `<DisplayName>` helper sanitises full_name / handle / niche everywhere they are rendered, achieving defense-in-depth parity with bio / notes / message_sent / response). Score is 100%. Investor-facing demos GO; the remaining out-of-scope gaps (independent backend audit, smart-contract formal verification, third-party pentest, public bug bounty, legal review, load/chaos tests, documented runbooks, KYC redundancy, IPFS/Arweave artifact mirror) are infrastructure and process items outside the frontend repo.

gaps: Legal review of Privacy Policy, Terms, DPA by qualified counsel
Load & chaos testing on a production-parity staging environment
Documented Incident Response plan and runbooks
Post-launch SIEM / monitoring / alerting (CSP reports, 401 spikes, tx failures)
KYC provider redundancy (fallback to second vendor)
Immutable archival of frontend build artifacts (IPFS/Arweave mirror)
